title: Katrina Manson on How AI is Changing the Nature of War - The Wire China
gist: In a Q&A on her book about Project Maven, Bloomberg's Katrina Manson traces how the Pentagon's flagship AI targeting system was born from anxiety over China's rising military, why battlefield AI accuracy collapses across geographies, how the program made Palantir a defense giant, and how U.S.-China friction has flipped Silicon Valley's stance on military work.

themes.theme: Project Maven origins and the China driver

insights.claim: Project Maven, launched by a 2017 Pentagon memo, was nominally about applying AI/computer vision to drone video feeds but was always intended to become an AI targeting effort, despite 'targeting' being omitted from even internal discussions for years.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: The gap between Maven's stated scope and its real targeting purpose shows how military AI is deliberately understated, a pattern for reading future programs.
insights.quote: ultimately it became an AI targeting effort.
insights.locator: Q: What is Project Maven

insights.claim: Deputy Defense Secretary Robert Work conceived AI as the third era of U.S. military dominance after the nuclear bomb and precision-guided weapons, viewing AI as a stepping stone to autonomy 'at rest' (digital mission control finding targets) and 'on the move' (AI on drones and unmanned fleets).
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Work's three-eras framing is the doctrinal logic behind sustained U.S. defense-AI spending; it explains why autonomy budgets compound regardless of administration.
insights.quote: the nuclear bomb...had kept the U.S. ahead. Then, precision-guided weapons...The third era, he thought, would be AI and autonomy.
insights.locator: It was Work’s vision that really enabled AI

insights.claim: Maven's genesis was driven by Robert Work's recognition that China had spent 10 years studying America's military weak points and was making U.S. aircraft carriers and other assets vulnerable.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Frames U.S. defense-AI as fundamentally a China-deterrence program, tying its funding trajectory to the Indo-Pacific threat picture.
insights.quote: He saw that China had been studying America’s weak points at war for 10 years and was beginning to find ways to circumvent America’s firepower.
insights.locator: The Deputy Defense Secretary at the time was Robert Work

insights.claim: The 'Breakfast Club'—a group of intelligence and defense higher-ups including Robert Work and Drew Cukor—originated Maven by working out how the U.S. should keep its technological edge and bring the military's culture up to speed on AI.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Shows defense-AI strategy was forged by a small informal elite, useful context for how rapidly priorities can shift with key personnel.
insights.quote: the Breakfast Club, which was a group of intelligence and defense higher-ups who came together to work out how the U.S. should keep its technological edge
insights.locator: Work would pop in on the Breakfast Club

themes.theme: Battlefield AI accuracy and its limits

insights.claim: Maven's algorithms, trained on Middle East desert drone footage, collapsed to 30 percent accuracy or lower when deployed in the green-background Philippines because the objects and environment differed.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Demonstrates military AI does not generalize across 'biomes', a hard constraint on how quickly it can be fielded against new theaters like Taiwan.
insights.quote: when they were used in the Philippines, they couldn’t recognize anything...the capabilities completely collapsed to 30 percent accuracy or lower.
insights.locator: What often happened, though

insights.claim: In Ukraine, Maven's algorithms initially couldn't recognize tanks, differentiate tanks with turrets blown off, or detect objects in snow; retraining on collected imagery of Russian tanks raised detection scores back up from 10-30 percent, but it took time.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Retraining lag means AI is least useful in the opening days of a war, a mission-critical weakness for any fast-moving Taiwan scenario.
insights.quote: Detection scores that had collapsed down to 10 to 30 percent started to come back up. But that took time.
insights.locator: So they brought over the satellites

insights.claim: Data labeling for Maven remains expensive and difficult—volunteer military labor failed (some scrawled expletives on imagery)—and the National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency now has a $700 million contract just for labeling.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Labeling is a persistent, high-value bottleneck and a real revenue line for data vendors in the defense-AI supply chain.
insights.quote: the [National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency, which oversees part of Project Maven] has a $700 million contract out just for labeling
insights.locator: Even today, there are problems with labeling

insights.claim: Since 2023 the U.S. trained algorithms to detect Chinese vessels well enough to demonstrate recognition of a Chinese destroyer to Joint Chiefs Chairman Dan Caine, but accuracy is still not where anyone would want despite enormous data collection.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Even the flagship China-detection use case remains immature, tempering claims that AI gives a near-term decisive Indo-Pacific edge.
insights.quote: we have a computer vision algorithm that can recognize a Chinese destroyer.
insights.locator: In the case of China and Taiwan that you mentioned

insights.claim: Drone-boat detection can fail from a single drop of ocean spray when the vessel has only one camera, implying multi-lens designs are needed for reliable autonomous tracking at sea.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: A concrete hardware design implication: sensor redundancy is a differentiator for autonomous naval systems, relevant for defense-hardware suppliers.
insights.quote: a drone boat that has only one lens is going to be more susceptible to ocean splashes than a drone boat with multiple lenses.
insights.locator: For some of the conditions under which the algorithms

insights.claim: The U.S. is developing autonomous armed jet skis (program codenamed Whiplash) to exploit its dominance in jet-ski production and offset China's shipbuilding advantage with an uncrewed weapons fleet.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Reveals an asymmetric U.S. autonomy bet that plays to domestic manufacturing strengths, a niche area for defense-tech investment.
insights.quote: The program is called Whiplash. I found it in Navy budget documents
insights.locator: What’s going on with AI jet skis?

insights.claim: The case for autonomy rests on Ukraine showing drones are susceptible to jamming (now run by fiber-optic cable), which is not expected to work in a Taiwan invasion scenario, feeding Admiral Paparo's 'Hellscape' concept of buying a month of time with mass autonomous systems.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Jamming resistance is the core technical driver of the autonomy push; it points to demand for comms-independent autonomous systems for an Indo-Pacific fight.
insights.quote: with enough autonomous systems on water, underwater and in the air, the U.S. could fend off an attack and buy him what he said is a month of time.
insights.locator: One thing that emerged clearly from Ukraine

insights.claim: The U.S. created a Defense Autonomous Warfare Group (DAWG) and in January launched a $100 million contest for voice-controlled autonomous drone-swarming tech, with many LLM companies submitting and working on it today.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: A concrete, funded pipeline connecting commercial LLM firms to autonomous-weapons development; a tradeable trend for defense-AI exposure.
insights.quote: they launched a hundred-million-dollar contest for voice-controlled autonomous drone swarming tech. Many of the LLM companies submitted for this
insights.locator: But it is within our imaginations now

insights.claim: The CIA smuggled rudimentary autonomous jet skis into Ukraine, and one washed up on Turkey's shores, raising the control problem of an armed autonomous vehicle that can be lost.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Illustrates the proliferation and loss-of-control risks of fielding armed autonomy, a governance overhang on the whole category.
insights.quote: a jet ski washed up on the shores of Turkey, sparking consternation inside the Pentagon
insights.locator: I also discovered that the CIA smuggled

insights.claim: Palantir, a struggling startup on its way out of the Defense Department when Maven began, was effectively forced by Colonel Cukor to build Maven's digital interface, and now owes its fortunes as a top defense contractor to Project Maven.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Maps the single program that made Palantir a defense giant, underscoring how government anchor contracts drive defense-AI equity value.
insights.quote: it’s entirely fair to say that Palantir owes its fortunes to Project Maven, certainly at the Defense Department.
insights.locator: One of your findings is that many now highly-recognizable AI companies

insights.claim: Maven Smart System is becoming a program of record by September 2026 with a dedicated congressional funding stream, is used in every U.S. combatant command, and NATO is adopting a version—money that flows to Palantir, which was once valued at over $400 billion.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Program-of-record status plus NATO adoption is a durable, multi-year revenue tailwind for Palantir; a concrete catalyst to track.
insights.quote: it’s going to become a program of record by September this year, which means it’s getting a dedicated funding stream
insights.locator: If you look at Maven Smart System today

insights.claim: Big-tech firms like AWS and Microsoft built the actual AI for Maven and treated it as a stepping stone to large Defense Department cloud contracts worth real money.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Shows Maven was a wedge into lucrative defense cloud deals, explaining why even the largest clouds competed for it.
insights.quote: Project Maven was a stepping stone to a big cloud contract in the Defense Department, and that was worth real money.
insights.locator: The actual AI was produced by companies like AWS and Microsoft

insights.claim: When Apple acquired the startup Xnor.ai—which had kept alive the algorithms Google originally developed for Maven—it stopped providing work for Project Maven, marking Apple as a company that declines Defense Department work.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Identifies Apple as an outlier opting out of defense AI, a differentiator in how to assess big-tech defense exposure.
insights.quote: But when Xnor was bought by Apple, it stopped providing work for Project Maven.
insights.locator: Apple is a very interesting case

insights.claim: U.S.-China friction has driven big-tech leadership to reverse its stance on military work, aided by a deliberate effort from the U.S. military and Congress to frame defense cooperation as 'the patriotic position'.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: The political reframing has unlocked big-tech defense participation, expanding the addressable market for military AI versus the 2018 Google-mutiny era.
insights.quote: A lot of U.S.-China friction has driven big tech leadership to change their position.
insights.locator: The relationship between the U.S. military and Silicon Valley has changed

insights.claim: Anthropic, sued after being labeled a supply-chain risk for refusing certain Pentagon work, is nonetheless used on classified networks and in U.S. operations in Iran—showing dissent is now about scope, not refusing the business of war.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Even AI labs with ethical lines are embedded in active operations, signaling military AI demand now reaches the frontier model providers.
insights.quote: even a company like Anthropic...is still being used on classified networks and is being used today in U.S. operations in Iran.
insights.locator: A lot of U.S.-China friction has driven big tech leadership

insights.claim: Tech-company workforces remain divided on defense work—hundreds of Google and DeepMind workers signed a protest letter against classified defense use—prompting firms to segment government-facing divisions staffed from the veteran community.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Internal labor friction is an ongoing operational risk and reputational variable for tech firms scaling defense work.
insights.quote: companies started to segment their work, creating sections of their companies that worked with the government and hired from the veteran community
insights.locator: If you look at the workforce, it’s different.

insights.claim: It took roughly a decade to get Maven adopted by Indo-Pacific Command, and Cukor always estimated it would take 20 years to change the U.S. military's bureaucracy and culture to embrace AI—putting the effort halfway through.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: The 20-year adoption horizon sets realistic expectations for how slowly military-AI capability actually fields, tempering near-term deterrence claims.
insights.quote: he always thought it would take 20 years for the U.S. military to change its bureaucracy, its culture and its idea of embracing AI.
insights.locator: Something retired Colonel Cukor told me stayed with me
